attributes.stem: A 42-year-old Aboriginal woman presents to the General Practice clinic. She is a community health worker who has been invited to participate in a research project examining diabetes prevalence in her remote community. The research team has approached her because of her connections within the community. She explains that while she believes the research could benefit her community, she is concerned about how the data will be used, who will have access to it, and whether the community will have any control over the findings. She asks for your opinion on the matter.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best addresses the principles of Indigenous data sovereignty in this context?
options: Recommending community governance over data collection, analysis and dissemination
Advising her that ethics approval ensures appropriate data management
Explaining that university researchers have ultimate ownership of collected data
Suggesting she request that all identifiable information be removed from the dataset
Encouraging her to seek legal advice about intellectual property rights
attributes.explanation:
This scenario highlights the importance of Indigenous data sovereignty, which recognises the right of Indigenous peoples to govern the collection, ownership, and application of data about their communities, peoples, lands, and resources.

Recommending community governance over data collection, analysis and dissemination is the most appropriate response as it aligns with the principles of Indigenous data sovereignty. This approach acknowledges that Indigenous communities should have the right to control how their data is collected, used, stored, and shared. It recognizes that data about Indigenous peoples belongs to the collective and should be used in ways that benefit the community. This approach would involve the community in all stages of the research process, from design to dissemination, ensuring that the research respects cultural protocols and Indigenous knowledge systems.

Advising her that ethics approval ensures appropriate data management is insufficient. While ethics approval is necessary, standard research ethics frameworks often do not adequately address Indigenous data sovereignty concerns. Traditional ethics processes may not recognise collective rights or cultural contexts specific to Indigenous communities. Ethics approval alone does not guarantee community control over data or ensure that research benefits flow back to the community.

Explaining that university researchers have ultimate ownership of collected data directly contradicts the principles of Indigenous data sovereignty. This approach perpetuates colonial power dynamics in research and ignores Indigenous peoples' rights to govern their own data.

Suggesting she request that all identifiable information be removed from the dataset addresses confidentiality but misses the broader issues of data sovereignty. De-identification does not address community ownership of data, control over how aggregate data is interpreted, or how findings are used. Indigenous data sovereignty extends beyond individual privacy to collective rights over community information.

Encouraging her to seek legal advice about intellectual property rights may be helpful but focuses too narrowly on Western legal frameworks that often do not recognize collective ownership or Indigenous knowledge systems. Indigenous data sovereignty requires approaches that honour Indigenous governance systems and cultural protocols, not just legal protections.

The CARE Principles for Indigenous Data Governance (Collective Benefit, Authority to Control, Responsibility, Ethics) provide guidance for research involving Indigenous communities. These principles complement the FAIR Principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) by centering Indigenous peoples' rights and interests in data. Implementing these principles would involve establishing formal agreements about data ownership, control, access, and possession before research begins, ensuring Indigenous governance throughout the research process, and guaranteeing that benefits from the research flow back to the community.
learningPoints: Indigenous data sovereignty recognises the right of Indigenous peoples to govern the collection, ownership, and application of data about their communities, peoples, lands, and resources.
Indigenous data governance frameworks should be developed in partnership with Indigenous communities to ensure cultural safety and respect for Indigenous knowledge systems.

The CARE Principles (Collective Benefit, Authority to Control, Responsibility, Ethics) provide guidance for Indigenous data governance alongside the FAIR Principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable).
references: Lowitja Institute. (2021). Indigenous data governance and sovereignty [Information sheet]. https://www.lowitja.org.au/page/tools/indigenous-data-sovereignty-readiness-assessment-and-evaluation-toolkit
